# Understanding anisotropy generated by fluctuations in heavy-ion collisions

> Event-by-event fluctuations are central to the current understanding of ultrarelativistic heavy-ion collisions. In particular, fluctuations in the geometry of the early-time collision system are responsible for new phenomena such as triangular flow, which have solved important puzzles in existing data. We propose a simple model where initial fluctuations stem from independent flux tubes randomly distributed in the transverse plane. We calculate analytically the moments of the initial anisotropies (dipole asymmetry, eccentricity, triangularity), which are the sources of anisotropic flow, and their mutual correlations. Our analytic results are in good agreement with calculations from commonly-used Monte-Carlo codes, providing a simple understanding of the fluctuations contained in these models. Any deviation from these results in future experimental data would thus indicate the presence of non-trivial correlations between the initial flux tubes and/or extra sources of fluctuations that are not present in current models.

# Understanding anisotropy generated by fluctuations in heavy-ion collisions

## Key claim

The paper proposes that initial-state anisotropies (dipole asymmetry, eccentricity, triangularity) in heavy-ion collisions can be understood from a simple model where fluctuations arise from independent, identical flux tubes randomly distributed in the transverse plane, and that any deviation of future experimental data from these analytic results would indicate non-trivial correlations between initial flux tubes or extra sources of fluctuations not present in current models.

## Method

The authors model the initial energy density as a superposition of N independent, identical sources (flux tubes) with a Gaussian profile, randomly distributed according to a smooth probability distribution. They derive analytic expressions for the moments of initial anisotropies ε₁, ε₂, and ε₃ (dipole asymmetry, eccentricity, triangularity) and their mutual correlations using a systematic expansion in powers of 1/N, where N is the number of sources. These analytic results are compared with Monte-Carlo calculations from both a Color-Glass-Condensate inspired model (MC-KLN) and a standard Monte-Carlo Glauber model for Pb-Pb collisions at 2.76 TeV.

## Key numbers

- The number of independent sources N is taken as N = 0.45 Npart for all centralities; increasing N by 20% typically decreases ε₃{2} and ε₁{2} by 10%, and ε₂{2} by less than 4%
- For central collisions where εs = 0, ε₃{2}² contains only the ⟨r⁶⟩ term while ε₁{2}² has an additional negative contribution proportional to ⟨r²⟩² - ⟨r⁴⟩, explaining why ε₁ < ε₃
- The ratio ε₁/ε₃ from the analytic model lies between 0.5 and 0.6 for both wounded nucleon and binary collision scaling, while Monte-Carlo gives values between 0.3-0.4 for wounded nucleons and 0.6-0.7 for binary collisions
- For the most central collisions, the ratio ⟨εₙ⁴⟩/⟨εₙ²⟩² is close to 2 for all n, corresponding to Gaussian fluctuations
- The four-particle cumulant ε₃{4}⁴ scales as 1/N³ for central collisions, with the explicit expression containing terms involving ⟨r⁶⟩, ⟨r¹²⟩, and ⟨r⁹⟩

## Context

This work extends earlier studies of event-by-event fluctuations in heavy-ion collisions, particularly the observation that fluctuations generate odd harmonics such as triangular flow (Alver, 2010) and the development of cumulant methods for initial anisotropies (Teaney and Yan, 2010). The independent-source model reproduces results from both CGC-inspired Monte-Carlo (MC-KLN) and Glauber Monte-Carlo calculations, providing a simple analytic understanding of the fluctuations contained in these complex models. The results enable direct comparison with experimental measurements of anisotropic flow harmonics and their correlations, and the authors note that the ratios ⟨εₙ⁴⟩/⟨εₙ²⟩² and angular correlations between event planes are independent of the number of sources to leading order in 1/N, making them robust observables for initial-state fluctuations.
